text:
실리콘 포토닉스 검사 수요 급증… 마텍(MA-tek)·범전(MSS), AI 광통신 시장 선점

$LITE, $AAOI, $COHR, $VIAV, $GLW

AI 데이터 센터가 고속 전송, 저전력, 고밀도 상호 연결 방향으로 발전함에 따라 실리콘 포토닉스(Silicon Photonics)가 반도체 산업의 핵심 기술로 떠오르고 있습니다. 데이터 센터의 속도와 방열 요구가 높아지면서 전자의 일부를 빛으로 대체하는 이 기술은 차세대 업그레이드의 필수 요소가 되었으며, 관련 검사 및 분석 장비 수요를 강력하게 견인하고 있습니다.

[마텍(MA-tek): 첨단 공정 분석의 선두주자]
마텍은 AI 칩, 자체 개발 ASIC, 실리콘 포토닉스 기술 개발에 힘입어 재료 분석(MA)과 고장 분석(FA) 수요가 크게 늘었다고 밝혔습니다.

특히 GAA(Gate-All-Around) 구조의 미세화로 인해 TEM(투과전자현미경), SIMS(이차이온질량분석기) 등 고해상도 검사 장비의 중요성이 커졌습니다.

첨단 패키징 과정에서 발생하는 복잡한 결함을 잡아내는 분석 역량을 바탕으로, 실리콘 포토닉스를 AI 및 첨단 공정과 맞물린 핵심 성장 동력으로 삼고 있습니다.

[범전(MSS): 독자 장비와 특허로 시장 공략]
범전은 실리콘 포토닉스 검사를 '제2의 성장 곡선'으로 정하고 실리콘 포토닉스 전담 엔지니어링 부서를 설립했습니다.

자체 개발한 실리콘 포토닉스 분석 장비인 'MSS HG' 3대를 완성했으며, 핵심인 광 손실 탐지 기술은 이미 대만, 일본, 미국에서 발명 특허를 획득했습니다.

위탁 검사부터 연구개발 테스트, 그리고 고객사에 직접 분석 장비를 도입하는 서비스까지 아우르는 통합 비즈니스 모델을 구축하여 시장 내 입지를 다지고 있습니다.

[분기 실적 현황]
두 기업 모두 올해 1분기 견조한 성장세를 보였습니다.

마텍: 3월 매출 5.38억 대만 달러를 기록했으며, 1분기 전체 매출은 14.24억 대만 달러로 전년 동기 대비 15.03% 증가했습니다.

범전: 3월 매출 2.24억 대만 달러를 기록했으며, 단월 주당순이익(EPS)은 0.35 대만 달러로 전년 대비 54.81% 급증했습니다.

text:
The photonics/optics sector (high-speed data comms, AI infrastructure) for names like $LWLG (electro-optic polymers) and $OPTX (components for comms/defense) is tied to tech capex cycles.

Failed US-Iran talks have spiked oil (Strait of Hormuz risks), adding energy cost pressure on data centers and broader risk-off flows that could trigger pullbacks in speculative growth plays.

Fed balance sheet expansion/liquidity injections remain supportive for risk assets, potentially cushioning valuations via easier money—though it risks inflating bubbles in already stretched areas.

Sector macro leans mixed short-term; watch oil and Fed signals for direction. NFA.

text: Nvidia’s adoption of Co-Packaged Optics (CPO) in its upcoming Vera Rubin AI chip platform hinges on the semiconductor testing supply chain, as the most difficult hurdle to mass production is wafer-level testing of the ‘Electronics-on-Top, Photonics-on-Bottom’ structure, which is a 3D stack of chips, like a sandwich, that uses TSMC’s SoIC advanced packaging technology, media report. The issue is testing for photonic and electrical signals differs significantly, and the industry does not have testing equipment able to do mass production volumes. If a solution cannot be found, it may hurt adoption of the technology. A number of companies are rushing to develop such equipment, including Advantest, Teradyne, FormFactor, MPI and Hermes Testing (HTSI). $NVDA $TER $FORM $6857 #semiconductors #semiconductor https://t.co/G1OGA9cBv9

text:
Great question.
$LWLG and $SIVE are both working in the photonics space, but they’re not really direct competitors. They’re actually attacking the same problem from different angles.
LWLG is focused on electro optic polymers. That’s a completely different material platform from traditional silicon or indium phosphide approaches. The big advantage they’re chasing is much lower power consumption, higher bandwidth, and easier integration into existing foundry flows without massive redesigns.
SIVE (and several other silicon photonics players) tends to stay within the silicon ecosystem. It’s more about refining what already exists at scale.
So in a way, they’re both pushing photonics forward, but LWLG is betting on a material shift while SIVE is optimizing within the current silicon paradigm. That’s why I see them as complementary rather than head to head rivals, at least for now.
The real question the market should be asking is which approach actually wins at scale in the AI data center world over the next 3-5 years. Polymers have some compelling long term advantages, but silicon has the manufacturing head start. interesting space to watch.

text:
Silicon Photonics Mass Production Hinges on Wafer Test Chain — 'Electrical-on-Top, Optical-on-Bottom' Architecture Is Key

As AI compute demand pushes high-speed transmission efficiency to its absolute limits, 2026 is widely regarded as the pivotal year for silicon photonics (SiPh) and co-packaged optics (CPO) to enter large-scale deployment — making it an urgent new battleground the industry is racing to stake out.

Notably, the success or failure of this silicon photonics mass production campaign now rests squarely on the shoulders of the semiconductor test supply chain.

According to OSAT industry sources, silicon photonics chip testing comprises three sequential process steps, and the most formidable production barrier currently lies at the wafer-level opto-electrical integrated test — referred to as the "electrical-on-top, optical-on-bottom" (上電下光) configuration. As this test sits at the second stage of the overall test insertion flow, the industry calls it "Insertion 2."

Specifically, because TSMC employs its SoIC technology to vertically stack the electronic IC (EIC) on top of the photonic IC (PIC), the resulting Compact Universal Photonic Engine (COUPE) must pass a dual-sided wafer test — electrical probing from the top, optical probing from the bottom — in order to screen known-good die from defective ones.

The core technical bottleneck is as follows: because the test conditions for optical and electrical signals differ significantly, no production-worthy automated test solution currently exists on the market.

For this reason, Insertion 2 has been dubbed a "black hole" in silicon photonics testing within the semiconductor industry — it is neither fast enough nor accurate enough.

In other words, how to develop a low-cost, high-efficiency automated test solution is intensely challenging the R&D capabilities of ATE (automated test equipment) vendors and test interface suppliers alike. If this bottleneck cannot be overcome to achieve economies of scale, it risks significantly dampening the market's willingness to adopt CPO in the near term.

Under mounting pressure from customers' production timelines, major test equipment players Advantest and Teradyne, along with leading test interface companies FormFactor, MPI Corporation (旺矽), and Hermes Testing (漢民測試), are accelerating the development of automated test solutions to clear the wafer-level opto-electrical integration hurdle and secure a first-mover position on this beachhead.

Additionally, supply chain sources note that the remaining two test steps for silicon photonics chips — "Insertion 1," corresponding to photonic IC testing, and "Insertion 3," corresponding to post-CPO-packaging final product testing — already have relatively mature automated solutions available on the market.

Regarding Insertion 1 product positioning, Advantest stated that "Triton," an integrated solution co-developed with FormFactor, is currently undergoing equipment qualification at customer sites and is expected to be formally deployed before year-end to support PIC testing in production.

For the critical mass production bottleneck at Insertion 2, the solution proposed by Advantest and its supply chain partners is expected to complete proof of concept (PoC) in the first half of the year, with the prospect of entering a joint development phase with customers in the second half.

Industry analysis indicates that silicon photonics chips face significantly elevated requirements for stability and accuracy in high-speed transmission environments, raising the overall test technology threshold in tandem. In particular, because the cost of detecting failures in silicon photonics chips after CPO packaging is completed is extremely high, Shift-Left Testing has become critically important — phased verification at earlier stages is now seen as the last line of defense for protecting margins.

However, supply chain sources revealed that if the electrical-on-top/optical-on-bottom test bottleneck fails to achieve an equipment-level breakthrough in a timely manner, test houses may opt to selectively skip Insertion 2 during initial mass production, betting instead on Insertion 3 where solutions are well-defined — a compromise that sacrifices some yield and cost efficiency in exchange for keeping final product shipment schedules on track.

"text": "Silicon Photonics Mass Production Hinges on Wafer Test Chain — 'Electrical-on-Top, Optical-on-Bottom' Architecture Is Key As AI compute demand pushes high-speed transmission efficiency to its absolute limits, 2026 is widely regarded as the pivotal year for silicon photonics (SiPh) and co-packaged optics (CPO) to enter large-scale deployment — making it an urgent new battleground the industry is racing to stake out. Notably, the success or failure of this silicon photonics mass production campaign now rests squarely on the shoulders of the semiconductor test supply chain. According to OSAT industry sources, silicon photonics chip testing comprises three sequential process steps, and the most formidable production barrier currently lies at the wafer-level opto-electrical integrated test — referred to as the "electrical-on-top, optical-on-bottom" (上電下光) configuration. As this test sits at the second stage of the overall test insertion flow, the industry calls it "Insertion 2." Specifically, because TSMC employs its SoIC technology to vertically stack the electronic IC (EIC) on top of the photonic IC (PIC), the resulting Compact Universal Photonic Engine (COUPE) must pass a dual-sided wafer test — electrical probing from the top, optical probing from the bottom — in order to screen known-good die from defective ones. The core technical bottleneck is as follows: because the test conditions for optical and electrical signals differ significantly, no production-worthy automated test solution currently exists on the market. For this reason, Insertion 2 has been dubbed a "black hole" in silicon photonics testing within the semiconductor industry — it is neither fast enough nor accurate enough. In other words, how to develop a low-cost, high-efficiency automated test solution is intensely challenging the R&D capabilities of ATE (automated test equipment) vendors and test interface suppliers alike. If this bottleneck cannot be overcome to achieve economies of scale, it risks significantly dampening the market's willingness to adopt CPO in the near term. Under mounting pressure from customers' production timelines, major test equipment players Advantest and Teradyne, along with leading test interface companies FormFactor, MPI Corporation (旺矽), and Hermes Testing (漢民測試), are accelerating the development of automated test solutions to clear the wafer-level opto-electrical integration hurdle and secure a first-mover position on this beachhead. Additionally, supply chain sources note that the remaining two test steps for silicon photonics chips — "Insertion 1," corresponding to photonic IC testing, and "Insertion 3," corresponding to post-CPO-packaging final product testing — already have relatively mature automated solutions available on the market. Regarding Insertion 1 product positioning, Advantest stated that "Triton," an integrated solution co-developed with FormFactor, is currently undergoing equipment qualification at customer sites and is expected to be formally deployed before year-end to support PIC testing in production. For the critical mass production bottleneck at Insertion 2, the solution proposed by Advantest and its supply chain partners is expected to complete proof of concept (PoC) in the first half of the year, with the prospect of entering a joint development phase with customers in the second half. Industry analysis indicates that silicon photonics chips face significantly elevated requirements for stability and accuracy in high-speed transmission environments, raising the overall test technology threshold in tandem. In particular, because the cost of detecting failures in silicon photonics chips after CPO packaging is completed is extremely high, Shift-Left Testing has become critically important — phased verification at earlier stages is now seen as the last line of defense for protecting margins. However, supply chain sources revealed that if the electrical-on-top/optical-on-bottom test bottleneck fails to achieve an equipment-level breakthrough in a timely manner, test houses may opt to selectively skip Insertion 2 during initial mass production, betting instead on Insertion 3 where solutions are well-defined — a compromise that sacrifices some yield and cost efficiency in exchange for keeping final product shipment schedules on track."}}}, "rest_id": "2043458181746315424", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "62651", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

BEYOND 1.6T - PHOTONICS ARCHITECTURE DISCOVERY REPORT

Three emerging architectures are mapped: 

CPO‑dominant clusters( $NVDA/ $AVGO/ $TSMC/ $LITE, $MRVL ‑Celestial), 

Optical circuit‑switched fabrics (LITE OCS, Google/Meta hyperscalers) that break switch‑radix limits.

Hybrid RF‑photonics edge/SatCom fabrics ( $SIVE, $MKSI, $HPK) linking 6G and satellites to core photonic data centers.

text:
As of April 15, 2026

The AI Photonics sector lit up today, led by $SIVEF and $SIVE ripping 35% and 30%. Sivers Semiconductors announced a collaboration with Jabil to develop a 1.6T LRO transceiver module using Sivers' DFB InP lasers for hyperscale AI datacenters at 2.5x lower power draw. (https://t.co/j4drYtj4on)

$ALMU's +16% layered on top, digesting its $4M+ U.S. government contract win from Monday. The Jabil deal confirms DFB laser sourcing as a critical bottleneck in the 800G-to-1.6T migration. Transceiver module names lagging on tariff overhang, not demand breakdown.

$SIVEF: +35.23% 
$SIVE: +30.20% 
$ALMU: +16.62% 
$IQE: +12.97% 
$SOI: +6.19% 
$SLOIF: +4.57% 
$POET: +3.87% 
$CIEN: +1.83%
$NDX: +1.40% 
$FORM: +1.04%

$MRVL: +0.58% 
$TER: -0.15%

$LWLG: -1.06% 
$AEHR: -1.56% 
$KEYS: -1.58% 
$COHR: -1.67% 
$BESIY: -1.81% 
$AAOI: -2.62% 
$GLW: -2.63% 
$LITE: -3.37% 
$VIAV: -3.73% 
$AXTI: -6.49%

1. Compound Semiconductor Substrates & Photonic Laser Sources

SIVEF, SIVE, IQE, SOI, SLOIF: Today's clear winner bucket. Jabil deal is a direct read-through for InP photonics suppliers, with the market pricing in compound semiconductor wafer tightness as 1.6T module demand scales into 2H26.

AXTI, TSEM, GFS: AXTI down 6.5% on its own supply-demand timing mismatch, not thesis deterioration. TSEM and GFS caught a modest SiPho/specialty foundry bid but lack the direct InP laser catalyst.

2. Photonic Integrated Circuits & Early-Stage Materials

ALMU: Carrying Monday's $4M+ government contract momentum into today. Quantum dot laser and nonlinear photonic materials funding is direct AI datacom and quantum compute positioning. Watch the AngelTech panel April 20.

POET, LWLG, BESIY: Risk-on sympathy trade. Today's catalyst is InP-specific, so EO polymer and other platform stories get lower beta. No fundamental change.

3. Optical Transceivers & Active Networking

CIEN: Steadiest name in the group, lower tariff exposure than pure module assemblers. AAOI, LITE, COHR, GLW, VIAV all red on supply chain repricing under current trade policy. Demand thesis intact.

4. Semiconductor Equipment, Test & Infrastructure

MRVL: Holding well. PAM4 DSP silicon wins regardless of which laser or module vendor takes the socket. AEHR, FORM, KEYS, TER soft across the board; no direct read-through from a laser collaboration print. KEYS' new 220 GHz lightwave analyzer is strategically relevant but not a near-term catalyst today.

of April 14, 2026 (removed some players from the list)

The AI photonics and optical infrastructure sector is showing a sharp split today, with substrate and materials plays surging while early-stage and speculative names get hammered. $SOI led the watchlist with an +11.91% move, extending a parabolic run fueled by accelerating investor conviction in Soitec's Photonics-SOI substrate business for co-packaged optics and AI datacenter interconnects. Morningstar noted that Soitec's shares rose 50% in just five trading sessions as investors priced in a bullish outlook for the firm's silicon photonics segment. Morningstar (https://t.co/djE1rX2T9F)
The broader sector is being reshaped by a clear bifurcation: capital is rotating into names with tangible substrate and test infrastructure exposure to the AI optical buildout, while pre-revenue photonic integration plays are getting punished. $POET dropped after Wolfpack Research disclosed a short position, calling the company "an obvious stock promotion" and flagging potential PFIC tax liabilities for U.S. shareholders ahead of the April 15 filing deadline. https://t.co/RRfdqNGIy4 $SIVEF fell in sympathy as the short report reinforced broader skepticism toward pre-revenue photonic chip names. Meanwhile, SPX +2.21% and NDX +1.66% confirm this is a risk-on tape where the photonics laggards are underperforming on idiosyncratic, not macro, weakness.

$ATEYY: +8.65%

$SIVEF: -11.50%
1. Compound Semiconductor Substrates and Foundries
$SOI, $BESIY, $TSEM, $AXTI: The substrate tier is where capital is concentrating. SOI's parabolic move reflects the market treating Photonics-SOI wafers as a structural bottleneck for CPO adoption. BESIY (BE Semiconductor) rode the same wave on die-attach and advanced packaging exposure. TSEM continues to benefit from specialty foundry demand, while AXTI held green on InP substrate scarcity narrative despite thin volume.
$IQE, $GFS: IQE gave back -2.65% as the OTC liquidity drag persists despite the same end-market tailwinds. GFS slipped -1.28%, still digesting the auto/industrial overhang even as its RF-SOI partnership with Soitec positions it well longer term.
2. Optical Transceivers and Active Networking
$COHR, $MRVL: Coherent and Marvell both green, tracking the 800G/1.6T transceiver cycle. COHR +1.80% is steady rather than explosive here, which suggests the easy money in pluggable transceiver names may already be priced.
$LITE, $CIEN, $AAOI: The transceiver and networking names that should be benefiting from the same AI optical build are instead lagging. LITE -2.15% and CIEN -2.68% are giving back recent gains. AAOI -4.41% continues to trade heavy, potentially reflecting profit-taking after its tariff-exemption-driven run.
$GLW, $VIAV: Passive optical infrastructure names drifting lower. Not broken, just not where the heat is today.
3. Semiconductor Equipment, Test and Infrastructure
$AEHR, $FORM, $KEYS, $TER, $ATEYY: AEHR +3.84% is the standout, continuing its recent strength on wafer-level burn-in demand tied to SiC and compound semi production ramps. ATEYY (Advantest) surged +8.65%, a strong signal that test infrastructure spending for advanced packaging and photonics ICs is accelerating. FORM flat, KEYS and TER slightly red but neither signaling anything concerning.
4. Early-Stage Photonic Materials and PICs
$LWLG, $ALMU, $POET, $SIVEF: LWLG bucked the early-stage selloff with a +7.20% move, potentially on renewed interest in EO polymer modulators as CPO architectures demand novel modulator solutions beyond conventional LiNbO3. ALMU -7.36% gave back gains on thin float dynamics. POET -8.01% is the direct casualty of the Wolfpack short report, which weaponized the PFIC tax angle right before the April 15 deadline. SIVEF -11.50% is the worst performer on the board, dragged down by contagion from the short seller thesis hitting the pre-revenue photonic integration cohort."}}}, "quotedRefResult": {"result": {"__typename": "Tweet", "rest_id": "2043803655737147597"}}, "rest_id": "2044145584949674252", "source": "<a href="https://mobile.twitter.com" rel="nofollow">Twitter Web App</a>", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "34827", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2044554804744360306", "source": "<a href="http://twitter.com/download/android" rel="nofollow">Twitter for Android</a>", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "28646", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
我理解的SIVE
聊完AEHR 和 AAOI 还有很大的涨幅空间
来聊聊目前上涨空间最大的一个公司SIVE
今天SIVE就+30%

SIVE

这个公司basically就是做让AI芯片传输数据的激光器
AI芯片是需要光用silicon photonics技术来传输数据的

为什么要用光来传输数据呢？
当电信号的频率极高的时候 电子不再在整根铜线内部跑 而是在铜线的表面跑 会让电阻指数级别飙升
数据中心的两张显卡交换数据的时候速度会达到1.6T
这么快的速度电信号跑不到1-2米就会衰减成没有办法识别的代码
而且为了对抗铜线的衰减，只能加大电压去推信号，产生的热量很高不仅费电还危险
最重要最重要的一点是光没有质量+没有电荷
在玻璃里传输的时候没有电阻
总结一句话来说就是
铜线的带宽是有极限的 而光的带宽是无限的
这也是为什么我前几年最大持仓的CRDO有压力的原因

在实验室做一个好激光器不难 但是难在100毫米的inp晶圆上 想量产laser arrays(我都不知道这个中文叫什么 like 激光器矩阵？somebody teach me）并且质量很高 是非常难的 所以大公司都找sive定

现在他们确定的订单有50M预定订单有450M
2028 2029年
我预估300M营收 x 20ps 5-6B的市值 这还是很保守的
今天市值才660M
660M x 10-15？

聊聊为什么还有很高空间 排除公司本身
1. 这个公司并没有在美国nasdaq上市
而是在瑞典nasdaq上市 来这边上市估计股价更高
想在fidelity上买还要加50🔪
2. 市值太小 like 660M的市值我都没办法布置sizeable 仓位 更别说其他manage billions的fund manager
3. 还有个x因素 x上一个很火的大神 @aleabitoreddit  粉丝量很大 分析ai公司非常厉害
我也在关注 好像他成sive 1%+的大股东了

我的预测 sive会被大公司收购 可能瞬间10x
我预估Avgo或者Marvell吧 这两家最有可能收购他 来补自己的短板

我的public accounts 持仓更新完整在图2:
Sive没有放进去因为大部分朋友自己的broker买不到
BE +53%
Aehr+59%
aaoi+74%
mu+30%
lwlg+45%
ichr+20%
uct+ 24%
等等

Join me
我所有持仓都将公开

text:
GAN is the way forward … lets discuss some alpha in the Photonics Supercycle: Signals in AIXA earnings call

$AIXA had earnings call and popped up another +11%, bringing YTD upside to +130% and 1 yr upside to +288%.

You guys know that am already invested in Aixtron ($AIXA) and have written about this German champion in the past, framing it as a quintessential "hidden monopoly" within the semiconductor equipment space. I also think that this is early innings of a massive Photonics Supercycle. As AI clusters migrate from 800G to 1.6T and eventually 3.2T optical modules, the bottleneck for AI scaling has shifted from the H100/B200 compute engine to the interconnect fabric itself. You cannot run a frontier model if the data can’t move between the GPU and the memory stack fast enough. Aixtron sits at the very mouth of this funnel.

I would not regurgitate the numbers here, rather analyse and unpack the things I consider important with impact on overall AI and semisector. 

The Great Divergence: GaN vs. SiC

One of the most tactical takeaways from the call was the stark contrast between Gallium Nitride (GaN) and Silicon Carbide (SiC).

🐊 SiC Overcapacity: Management was candid about Silicon Carbide being in a state of "substantial market overcapacity," with SiC expected to be only 10% of their revenue this year. This is a warning shot for pure-play SiC substrate and device makers who are still digesting inventory.

🐊 The GaN Opportunity: Conversely, GaN is entering a golden era. Management expects AI data centers to become the largest single application for GaN power semiconductors. Because GaN is significantly more efficient than traditional silicon at high frequencies, it is becoming the standard for the power supply units (PSUs) that feed power-hungry AI servers.

For those looking to play the GaN trend beyond the equipment layer, keep an eye on these players:

• Navitas Semiconductor ($NVTS) – A US-based pure-play leader in GaN power ICs.

• Infineon Technologies ($IFX.DE / $IFNNY) – A European giant with a massive, growing footprint in GaN following their acquisition of GaN Systems.

• STMicroelectronics ($STM) – Another European heavyweight with strong GaN-on-Silicon capabilities.

• BluGlass ($BLG.AX) – An Australian-listed speculative play involved in specialized

🐊 Datacom Doubling: Management is forecasting that demand for datacom lasers will more than double YoY in 2026. This is a massive green flag for the laser makers themselves, such as Lumentum ($LITE) and Coherent ($COHR), who are racing to expand Indium Phosphide (InP) capacity. (am invested in both)

🐊The "Tool of Record" for AI Interconnects

The standout signal from the call was the performance of the G10-AsP system. Management confirmed it has become the "tool of record" for the next generation of photonic components

🐊 Order Intake Explosion: Order intake hit €171 million, a ~30% YoY increase that blew past analyst expectations. Crucially, 65% of these orders came from the optoelectronics segment

🐊Implications for the Memory (HBM) Ecosystem

While we often view memory through the lens of SK Hynix or Micron $MU, the Aixtron update provides a critical "read-through" for how these chips will be connected in the future.

🐊 Structural Bottlenecks: For those holding SK Hynix, $DRAM or Micron ($MU), the shift toward optical interconnects is the solution to the "power wall" that threatens to limit HBM performance. Aixtron’s order backlog suggests the equipment for this transition is being installed now.

🐊 Near-Stack Integration: The acceleration of Co-Packaged Optics (CPO) means that optical engines will eventually sit directly on the package alongside High-Bandwidth Memory (HBM) stacks.

Look the "Photonics Supercycle" isn't a theory anymore it’s appearing in the capex cycles of the world's largest chipmakers.

And remember, none of this is financial advice. The crocodile can make errors. So do your own research.

text:
$SIVE

I just found out about this.

I only analyzed the photonics part.

Are you telling me that my largest position combines photonics (the booming sector) and space (the next booming sector)?

Come on.

I’m not selling anything until a 10x at the very least.

Everyone talks about $SIVE and AI photonics.

But few talk about this:
They lost a SATCOM RFQ to MixComm… then acquired them a year later.

Now they sit across: 
• AI compute (photonics) 
• SATCOM/Wireless (defense, satellite, 5G/6G, DoD) 

Two mega trends. One story.

Mais ils ont aussi un segment communications optiques. Pas l'ASML de la photonique mais une boite qui pourrait se rerater rapidement. D'ailleurs hier il y a eu un afflux plutôt anormal, ce qui me fait potentiellement penser à un positionnement d’un bon gros player. text:
✏️LESSON EXTRACTION FROM SUCCESSFUL PITCH

Soitec ( $SOI.PA) by @jasons_chips 

121% gain since the March 10 pitch.

📋 WHAT THE PITCH ARGUED:
🔹 Mobile segment was at a cyclical floor with inventory clearing.
🔹 "Smart Cut" technology created a near-monopoly in photonics substrates.
🔹 Management was deliberately low-balling future growth projections.

✅ WHAT ACTUALLY HAPPENED:
🔹 Q3 sequential revenue beat confirmed the mobile trough was in.
🔹 1.6T networking demand pulled photonics orders forward significantly.

🎯 THE SURPRISE:
The market re-rated the stock as a pure AI infrastructure play almost overnight. It shifted from a "boring" materials cyclical to a critical networking bottleneck provider.

MY TAKE: We expected a slow recovery as inventory cleared. Instead, NVIDIA's commentary on Co-Packaged Optics triggered a massive institutional rotation into the name. This shows how quickly a value setup turns into a momentum play when the macro tailwind arrives.
But if I am honest, this performance is likely a result of important people mentioning this company on X 😀

user_name: Phillip, These are my opinion. Audi Alteram Partem
text: Love the pride in SA science, but calling this "the fastest Internet in the world" is talking kak. Prof Forbes' group does excellent quantum photonics research, but we're still years (maybe decades) from practical long-distance entangled-photon internet. Distance, loss, and the need for cryogenically cooled detectors are massive unsolved problems. It's lab stuff, not deployed tech.

text: Fiber laser with anti-collision system made in Poland. Kimla builds their fiber lasers using US/German supplier IPG Photonics for the source. Most of the machine is from components made in Poland. It's actually clean, it doesn't fund the Chinese.
embedded_text:
Building a fast fiber laser is easy
protecting it from collisions at  speed is the actual problem

Kimla Anti collision system reacts 20X faster than the competition 

linear motors + height sensors that doesnt lag speed 
without safety is just expensive downtime simply waiting https://t.co/e6MNiVB5wO

text: @ParadisLabs @ParadisLabs I've actually been doing quite a bit of research in the overall photonics sector (outside of Data Center/AI). $JEN came up in my research, I can send over my write-up if you would like. Check DMs.

@PhotonicsTrackr @PepInvestStocks Yeah $LASE doesn’t really fit the layer taxonomy - they’re an end-application laser play (industrial cutting/welding/marking) rather than a supplier into the photonics stack. Interesting angle is the pivot into defense/directed energy.

@mattpurdy89 @PepInvestStocks $LASE is interesting- we didn’t include it in our layer mapping. It does call itself a photonics-based industrial products company but we couldn’t find any evidence of it fitting into our layer taxonomy. 

Curious about your thoughts. https://t.co/JHPdDb4P43

text:
I'm confused how Jenoptik, $JEN, is up 65% YTD?

The "Photonics" label is doing a lot of heavy lifting for some pretty shaky fundamentals:

-> $JEN is a Tier 2 supplier, but getting priced like a monopoly. High capex + R&D exposure without pricing power of true lithography giants like $ASML

-> ​€100M capex on their new fab is just going to kill margins. With the semi-recovery looking lumpy in Q2 2026, the fixed cost drag could lead to a huge earnings miss by end of yr.
​
-> ​26x P/E for a business that is still 30% low-margin traffic safety and automotive metrology. The "pure photonics" rebrand is a marketing ploy, and not a financial or operational reality yet.

-> In the Photonics segment, Chinese competitors are eroding the moat in micro-optics and laser modules. $JEN is being squeezed between the high-end (Zeiss) and the low-cost (MLOptic).

$JEN is just a cyclical hardware firm w/ significant non-core baggage being priced like a secular AI winner.

Everyone talks about $SIVE and AI photonics.

But few talk about this:
They lost a SATCOM RFQ to MixComm… then acquired them a year later.

Now they sit across: 
• AI compute (photonics) 
• SATCOM/Wireless (defense, satellite, 5G/6G, DoD) 

Two mega trends. One story.

text:
The shift to Glass Substrates and Co-Packaged Optics is the biggest infrastructure pivot in a decade. If you trade AI, semicap, or photonics, this is required reading.

https://t.co/HjI1pTeIX0

$LPKF is interesting because it sits at the enabling layer of this transition. Its LIDE and glass-substrate systems are designed for defect-free through-glass vials, precise glass structuring, and production-scale advanced packaging, exactly the sort of process capability glass-core substrates need if they are going to matter in AI data centers and heterogeneous integration.

What makes this different from a normal “new tech” story is that the entire stack is moving at once. Hyperscalers want lower power, higher bandwidth, and tighter integration, and that pushes the industry toward optical I/O, co-packaged optics, and glass-based interposers that can support cleaner signal paths and better manufacturing scalability.

$LPKF is the machinery story. It is the precision laser-processing and glass-structuring pick that benefits if glass substrates become a standard platform rather than a niche experiment. The market often prices the chip designers and optics vendors first, but the equipment and process layer can be just as important once adoption starts.

Then you have the broader photonics names that represent different parts of the buildout. Sivers Semiconductors $SIVE operates in Wireless and Photonics, with photonics products tied to optical communication, sensing, and optical wireless networks, so it is exposed to the same bandwidth and light-based connectivity trend that supports CPO.

$IQE is another name that matters because it manufactures compound semiconductor materials and epitaxial wafers for photonics applications, including visible and infrared materials used in sensing and communications. That makes IQE part of the upstream materials layer that optical systems depend on, especially where compound semiconductors and III-V materials are central.

$POET is a more direct photonic-integration story because it is built around the POET Optical Interposer, a platform meant to integrate electronic and photonic devices into a single multi-chip module using wafer-level manufacturing and packaging. That positions POET squarely inside the AI/datacenter optical-connectivity narrative rather than adjacent to it.

$LITE and $COHR are the more established photonics names, and they matter because they make lasers, optical subsystems, and photonic technologies that power AI and cloud infrastructure. Nvidia’s recent strategic investment in both companies underscores how serious the industry is about securing next-generation optical supply for AI infrastructure.

text: @eyesigh558 Mobile is 52% of revenue and was down 36% year over year last quarter. That is exactly why the stock is down 75% from its highs and why the photonics segment is being ignored. The mobile downcycle is the noise

People are shifting to majors in $AMZN $GOOG $NVDA and also in photonics they are moving towards the majors... $CLS $COHR and $CREDO 

You can see it. People are rushing to secure gains in photonics but don't want to leave the sector of semi's. 

High beta options might struggle that have ran 100-200%+

$ALAB and $APH still have room.

text:
FinX has been so consumed with the likes of 

$SOFI $HOOD $HIMS $ZETA $NBIS $IREN and the such 

That it completely missed out on the photonics plays bottlenecking AI in $LITE $COHR $AAOI $MRVL

Nokia’s bull thesis (as of April 2026): $NOK

The $2.3B Infinera acquisition has created a vertically integrated optical/photonics powerhouse addressing AI data center bottlenecks with high-speed coherent pluggables (1.6T+), lower power, and scalable DCI fabrics. 

OFC 2026 demos and strong hyperscaler orders (+17% optical growth) signal acceleration in the 11–18% CAGR interconnect market.

Combined with NVIDIA’s $1B investment and AI-RAN trials (T-Mobile, SoftBank), Nokia builds end-to-end AI-native networks from wireless edge (RAN leadership + 6G foundations) to optical core—few peers match this stack.

2026 guidance provides a floor; optical/IP ramps (10–12%+ potential) plus margin expansion could drive 10–15%+ EPS growth and rerating to growth multiples.

I’m seeing some pushback on an equipment level, and I want to explain a little bit on that. @vlmkapital wrote a good post on how he views the investment case from the equipement level (link in comments) and I want to expand on things there are missing. Heads up.. this one is longer than usual. Most of the pushback I’m seeing X on sectors is superficial, and the argument I’m making here has multiple layers that all depend on each other. So if you want the short version, you won’t find it here💁‍♂️ @GyujinAAIG is working on the full scope for AAIG, and will publish the research soon but I will lay out the strategy part from my side already a bit. The lens that is being used when tracking companies like LPKF and the timelines is a technology adoption kind of lens. Will give @FinnStockinger also a tag in this part. But they’re missing the capital allocation and incentive lens, which is freaking crucial for this thesis. I’m going to explain a little bit why, and later I’m also going to use Aehr Test Systems as some kind of example of what happened there. So what’s the situation on the ground now? Semco expects the glass substrate era to enter into full swing around 2027-ish. They are currently developing samples with customers, and Absolics is also targeting mass production in 2027 and moving ahead with preparation. They got SKC approval for a capital increase of around $690 million, allocating roughly $410 million into Absolics for product development. So there are real commitments there, no doubt. Also, I’m not saying to totally ignore the glass thesis. We are watching it closely as well. I’m just pointing out some crucial things. If you look carefully at what is actually being built, Absolics is building a facility of around 12,000 square meters and is in some pre-qualification discussions with AMD and AWS. That’s not HVM. That is a very expensive demonstration phase, which is totally normal. It happens a lot. Companies build pilot lines, run sample production, and do customer qualification runs. It’s an investment to stay in the technology race, but it’s not revenue-generating HVM deployment. Keep that in mind. The reality is that it’s a qualification facility running at a small fraction of the scale needed to move the industry and the economics. I will elaborate further on that a bit later. Semco and Absolics are substrate manufacturers, and they’re building capacity for a market whose architectural gateway has not yet opened. It actually has been delayed. And this is where people keep getting confused — substrate manufacturer ramp-up plans are not the same thing as equipment-supplier inflection. SEMCO and Absolics don’t decide whether glass enters the HVM stack at scale. They’re positioning themselves for it. Two very different things."}}}, "rest_id": "2045604137866551549", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "14998", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2045611455228047700", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "3034", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

39 M$ de Capitalisation boursière >Elle est directement en rapport avec la photonique, elle fabrique des atténuateurs Optiques Variables (VOA) MEMSCAP, à la base, c’est surtout un fournisseur de capteurs de pression de très haute précision pour l’aéronautique et le médical. Mais ils ont aussi un segment communications optiques. Ils fournissent des VOA basés sur technologie MEMS, utilisés pour gérer dynamiquement la puissance des signaux dans les réseaux fibre optique (datacenters, télécoms, amplis EDFA). Sur ce segment de niche, MEMSCAP est tout simplement leader mondial des puces MEMS VOA, avec une part de marché estimée à plus de 80% sur un marché d’environ 5 M$ au niveau puces, et 50 M$ au niveau produits finis. Pas l'ASML de la photonique mais une boite qui pourrait se rerater rapidement. D'ailleurs hier il y a eu un afflux plutôt anormal, ce qui me fait potentiellement penser à un positionnement d’un bon gros player. Si les microcaps continuent d'exploser, je parie que Memscap en bénéficiera probablement."}}}, "rest_id": "2045528106413199710", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "181834", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2045589392387920373", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "1589", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

Added $SIVE (Sivers Photonics) and $IQE to the list.

Top 3 most likely to 10x (small mcap vs photonics peers like Lumentum/Coherent + AI/data center tailwinds for compound semis/optics):

1. $SIVE (~$370M USD) - laser/photonics ICs for AI interconnects.
2. $IQE (~$460M USD) - epi wafers, dedicated photonics segment.
3. $ALRIB (~$200M USD) - MBE equipment enabling next-gen photonics production.

Others more tangential (testing/networking/power). NFA, DYOR.

text: I think everyone is thinking about tesla / spacex /Xai advanced technology fab and terafab wrong. IMO the ATF is going to be a greenfield project, quickly getting 2nm production running, but then in parallel, be introducing photonics testing as quickly as possible.  The goal is to rapidly test and iterate each of these as quickly as possible, so by 2028 or 2029 they are at full photonic computing, at this point they outfit terrafab without the need for many EUV machines. Full photonics are incredible for orbital data centers with much lower weight, power requirements, radiation hardening and emp immunity.

text:
When every photonics name has been pumped to their absolute limits. There is only one place left to rotate. The material layer. And I'll be the one winning.

Great team, great company.   Dust designs photonic ICs (PICs) and engines that are foundational to Silicon Photonics. Silicon Photonic pluggables integrate multiple optical functions – traditionally", "id_str": "2044410644301046031", "is_quote_status": false, "lang": "en", "quote_count": 17, "reply_count": 26, "retweet_count": 33, "retweeted": false, "user_id_str": "340387261"}, "note_tweet": {"is_expandable": true, "note_tweet_results": {"result": {"entity_set": {"hashtags": [], "symbols": [], "urls": [], "user_mentions": [{"id_str": "1393596223433711616", "indices": [26, 39], "name": "Atreides Management, LP", "screen_name": "atreidesmgmt"}]}, "id": "Tm90ZVR3ZWV0OjIwNDQ0MTA2NDM5NDQ1NDIyMDg=", "text": "Nice to see Credo acquire @atreidesmgmt portfolio company DustPhotonics. Great team, great company.   Dust designs photonic ICs (PICs) and engines that are foundational to Silicon Photonics. Silicon Photonic pluggables integrate multiple optical functions – traditionally implemented with discrete components – into a single component called a PIC. This saves space and power while improving scalability.     Perhaps most importantly, PICs can feed multiple optical lanes with a smaller number of lasers, relative to traditional approaches typically requiring a discrete laser for each lane (e.g., 8x200G lasers for a 1.6T module). Timely given we are heading into a severe shortage of lasers. Please note that there will still be a laser shortage even if Dust’s PICs are broadly adopted.   Thermal and power-density limitations for pluggables will also eventually make adding more and more lasers impractical.  Silicon Photonics and PICs essentially create another axis for scaling bandwidth. And obviously, long-term, Silicon Photonics is essential for CPO.   PICs feel similar to RF in the early days of cellular - quite a bit of black magic. There are only a few companies successfully supplying datacom PICs today at scale – Dust is one of them.     Ronnen and team have been excellent operators and partners, and their products are well respected across the datacom ecosystem. Over $500m in optical revenue for Credo is material and I do think HyperLume was a smart bet for them - starting to hear more positive feedback about MicroLEDs from our venture portcos and the hyperscalers. Copper, CPO, Pluggables, MicroLEDs should all win for the foreseeable future in different applications in different hyperscaler networking topologies: networking is taking huge share of the datacenter BoM everywhere.   Coherent training clusters are increasing in size to enable ever larger models. A larger coherent cluster is much more networking intensive. And then the larger models trained on these larger clusters require larger switched scale-up domains to inference economically, which is again more networking intensive. Rubin and Trainium 4 will have much larger switched scale-up domains and we may need these systems deployed at scale to enable the broad availability of 10 trillion plus parameter models like Mythos. Networking, especially switched scale-up networking, should be the fastest growing part of the datacenter for the next several years.   Switched scale-up networking (almost all copper with some optical beginning late next year) > scale across (optical obviously) > scale-out (first place for CPO) from a growth perspective next 3 years imo. We will be using copper well into the 2030s and somewhat ironic that the growth of optical is likely to drive accelerated growth for copper in the near term relative to the strange zero-sum thinking I occasionally see here and in some sell-side notes."}}}, "rest_id": "2044410644301046031", "source": "Twitter for iPad", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "120286", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2044492573645869108", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "317", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
On $CRDO's acquisition of Dust Photonics.

The $TSEM foundry thesis is gaining traction, and it makes sense given Tower's SiPh capabilities.

Worth adding: $ALAB did the same thing last October, acquiring aiXscale Photonics. A copper-first interconnect company buying a small photonics startup to get into optics. Both companies made the same call.

I haven't seen anyone put these two side by side yet.

https://t.co/6ZZhAm3XsA
embedded_text:
Excellent episode. Don't feel bad if you didn't know about Dust Photonics pre-deal. They said $TSEM was probably the fab for Dust, so if $CRDO blows out the product, it is incremental demand for TSEM. Also, an interesting liquid cooling angle.

As it comes to CPU, my @openclaw

Everyone talks about $SIVE and AI photonics.

But few talk about this:
They lost a SATCOM RFQ to MixComm… then acquired them a year later.

Now they sit across: 
• AI compute (photonics) 
• SATCOM/Wireless (defense, satellite, 5G/6G, DoD) 

Two mega trends. One story.

text:
源杰科技（https://t.co/ZBjZH3oFsS）是激光芯片IDM厂商，2013年1月由张欣刚（ZHANG XINGANG，美国籍，1970年生）在陕西咸阳创立。

他清华本科、南加州大学材料科学背景，曾任Luminent研发经理、Source Photonics研发总监。现任董事长兼总经理。

妹妹张欣颖任董事（持股约3.43%）。核心技术副总潘彦廷等。

主营2.5G-50G磷化铟激光芯片，用于数据中心、5G、FTTH等。2022年科创板上市，近期AI算力需求推高股价超茅台成A股新股王。官网：https://t.co/ekEJYAQGbk

The photonics move has been sharp. A lot of names already up big.

Still watching $LITE, $AAOI and $SMTC for cleaner entries. Rather buy consolidation than chase strength.
The AI networking theme isn't done. Just waiting for the right setup.

Anyone else still in this space? https://t.co/CrtRZYLS0I

text:
CRDO가 DustPhotonics(이름이 왜 이래😊)를 인수한 내용을 Astera Labs가 aiXscale Photonics 인수한 내용과 버무려 글을 써봤습니다.

DustPhotonics의 광학 역량을 직접 확인하고 싶어서 역량껏 찾아봤는데, 학회 논문도 없고 coupling loss 수치도 미공개라 외부 검증에는 실패했습니다. 좀 아쉽지만 그 자체가 하나의 데이터 포인트라고 생각해서 솔직하게 다뤄봤습니다.

그리고 제가 생각한 진짜 수혜 레이어는 칩을 설계한 회사도, 칩을 산 회사도 아닙니다. 그 칩을 찍는 Foundry예요. 이걸 통해서 풀어봤습니다. paywall 뒤 내용인데 이 정도는 공개합니다😅

다들 CRDO 주가, 인수 사실에만 집중하던데, 공급망 구조로 읽으면 완전히 다른 회사가 보일 것입니다.

A $750M SiPh acquisition deal just dropped.
But the company that benefits most doesn't even appear in the headlines.

$CRDO is acquiring Israeli fabless SiPh PIC company DustPhotonics for $750M cash upfront. Up to ~$1.3B including earnout.
What this article covers: https://t.co/ezL42jZ1HD

But the company that benefits most doesn't even appear in the headlines. $CRDO is acquiring Israeli fabless SiPh PIC company DustPhotonics for $750M cash upfront. Up to ~$1.3B including earnout. What this article covers: DustPhotonics' core L3C technology. Known specs exist, but coupling loss, alignment tolerance, and other key performance data have never been disclosed. No peer-reviewed device papers at any major conference. Not saying the tech is bad. Saying independent verification isn't possible. $ALAB acquired aiXscale Photonics in October 2025. Credo bought the PIC chip. Astera bought the fiber-chip coupling interface. Different value chain positions, same direction. Electrical connectivity companies are buying their way into optics. There's a missing variable here. DustPhotonics is fabless. They've been fabricating wafers at one specific foundry for five years. SiPh PDK switching costs are far higher than CMOS. Where does this optical internalization trend show up in the numbers first? But the company that benefits most doesn't even appear in the headlines. $CRDO is acquiring Israeli fabless SiPh PIC company DustPhotonics for $750M cash upfront. Up to ~$1.3B including earnout. What this article covers: DustPhotonics' core L3C technology. Known specs exist, but coupling loss, alignment tolerance, and other key performance data have never been disclosed. No peer-reviewed device papers at any major conference. Not saying the tech is bad. Saying independent verification isn't possible. $ALAB acquired aiXscale Photonics in October 2025. Credo bought the PIC chip. Astera bought the fiber-chip coupling interface. Different value chain positions, same direction. Electrical connectivity companies are buying their way into optics. There's a missing variable here. DustPhotonics is fabless. They've been fabricating wafers at one specific foundry for five years. SiPh PDK switching costs are far higher than CMOS. Where does this optical internalization trend show up in the numbers first? text:
Copper wires are hitting a physical wall.
As #AI models demand increases in data transfer speeds between chips, electrical resistance is becoming an insurmountable barrier.
Enter Silicon Photonics: integration of laser-driven optical comms directly onto silicon substrates. https://t.co/HmVcjDHm7G

text: Being up 50% doesn’t mean fully rerated. TSEM for example still trades at a significant discount to Western peers despite being a key player in the silicon photonics supply chain, the market hasn’t fully priced its positioning yet. Even large caps like Marvell aren’t fully rerated, the market is still pricing it as a networking chip company, not as a custom AI silicon platform. That re-rating alone is worth multiple turns on the multiple. Same logic applies to smaller names like POET, where the technology is real but institutional coverage is near zero. We’re still early on most of these

text:
Big day for Soitec! 

Up a solid 40% since we profiled it. 

A lot of the recent move is due to retail climbing into the hype around Photonics following @aleabitoreddit and @citrini being bullish on the stock.

Of all the photonics names, this probably remains my favorite reward to risk. 

Given the moat. The bombed out valuation from other segments (which will inflect soon), and the partnerships the company has with players like Shin Etsu.

Excited to hold this for longer and watch the thesis play out. 

I’m used to waiting much longer for a thesis to play out- and let it be known that we haven’t fundamentally seen it played out as of yet.

Thank you to the hive mind X community we have so many experts bringing these names to light!

text:
I’m really like that man

I top ticked OXY to the day boy. My ytd rivals all those accounts that been spamming Photonics and all those AI supply chain stocks. Yet I never owned any of those AI plays. Missing out on Bloom Energy hurts my heart after pumping it earlier much in the year. I could have been talking more shit against all those AI guys if I longed it but it’s whatever 😂, being able to have a return match the AI peddlers on this platform without any of their AI exposure, feels good in it of itself. I don’t have Serenity’s return though that dude is killing it, but I legit have zero influence in being able to pump stocks. If people actually listened to me and followed my plays I would crush his return too I believe 😂. Some can be turned off by my competitiveness but this is a zero sum game. Kill or be eaten. I didn’t make the rules, but ima play the game to not just win, but to dominate. I only feel good when it’s a blow out.

text:
The 3-year rule holds for commoditizable bottlenecks: DRAM, NAND, legacy logic. It breaks for capital-intensive or IP-protected ones: ASML EUV is 9 years and counting, TSMC leading-edge over a decade, Soitec/Shin-Etsu on SOI wafers 15+ years. Photonics supply chain fits the second bucket, not the first.

On 'meme-like' valuations, the right benchmark isn't Street targets, it's what the market already pays for rerated peers. Coherent trades at ~8.4x EV/Sales, growing 22% Y/Y, with Nvidia owning $2B of equity. That's the anchor for a fully-discovered photonics name this cycle.

Checking the supply chain against it: Soitec ~4x EV/Sales (50% discount to COHR), IQE ~6x (in-play takeover), LPKF ~2x. Those are meaningful discounts to the rerated comp, not premiums.

Where you're right: names that have run 500%+ in months on sub-$50M revenue can't be defended on peer-relative grounds either. Those are real outliers worth separating from the broader supply-chain story.

Duration holds, most of the supply chain trades at discounts to the rerated peer, some individual names are genuinely overextended. Bottleneck durability and single-name multiples aren't the same question.

text:
Most finance Twitter talks about semiconductors.

Almost nobody on finance Twitter has actually built one.

Trade Whisperer has 21 years in HBM, DRAM, and NAND plus graduate research in silicon photonics and lasers.

His AMA is open right now.

If you have any real exposure to $MU, $SNDK, $TSM, or the broader semi complex, this is a rare window.

Here's why it matters.

The memory thesis driving Micron's projected out-earning of Amazon and Meta by 2027 sits on one question.

Can Samsung, SK Hynix, and Micron ramp HBM supply fast enough to capture current AI demand without triggering the classic memory glut cycle?

Analysts have views. Sell-side has models. Bulls and bears argue on podcasts.

Almost none of them have ever been inside a fab.

Trade Whisperer answers questions like "how long does HBM qualification actually take" and "why can't China catch up" from the engineering floor rather than the spreadsheet.

From the thread already running:

China catching up to HBM is "extremely hard. It's a moving target. If they ever figure out HBM3, others will be producing HBM4E by then."

Fab expansions "take decades for 100% utilization." The supply glut fear is priced for a cycle that can't physically happen as fast as the market assumes.

He's bullish on SK Hynix over Samsung specifically because of HBM3E leadership. That's not a trading view. That's an engineer's view on who actually makes the best chip right now.

These are the kind of reads that generate edge in semi positions.

Not because he's predicting prices. Because he's explaining physical constraints that determine what the prices eventually have to reflect.

If you hold MU or SNDK or any semi name, spend 20 minutes reading his replies.

Ask him something specific. Depth of qualification cycles. HBM4 timeline. Silicon photonics for interconnect. How packaging capacity actually constrains supply.

Finance Twitter is full of people who read earnings transcripts.

A semi engineer with 21 years of HBM experience doing an AMA is a different tier of information.

Use it while it's open.

Follow for more signals on when someone who actually knows something is worth listening to in public.

text:
Totally agree, photonics looks set to shine bright in the 2030s! 🚀
I love how it can be made using the same manufacturing lines as regular computer chips. That makes scaling up much easier. And combining it with terahertz tech on silicon for computing, communication, and even imaging all in one? Sounds like a powerful way forward.  Any interesting recent papers on this terahertz + photonics combo you’d recommend? Always fun chatting with someone who sees the potential too! ✨

text: @JackXJong Fair question. GlobalFoundries handles their wireless products, while WIN Semiconductors is used for high-volume photonics production, such as DFB lasers and arrays. The Glasgow fab is mainly focused on low-volume manufacturing, pilot runs, and continued technology development.

It is the company’s 100mm InP fab in Glasgow 🏭 Sivers owns and operates one of the few independent III-V photonic foundries with both advanced development capability and meaningful production capacity. This is not just an R&D line, but a qualified InP100 manufacturing platform with end-to-end capabilities — from epitaxy design and wafer processing to automated testing and qualification. The platform includes:  • Up to 5,000 wafer starts per year  • Automated test capacity of more than 2 million lasers per month  • ~$17M capex to expand toward ~$150M annual revenue capacity What makes this especially interesting is the etched-facet process with on-wafer optical coatings and testing. Rather than relying on slower, less efficient post-cleave workflows, Sivers can process, coat, and test on the full wafer before dicing — improving yield, cost, cycle time, and precision. Strategically, that matters. The in-house fab gives Sivers control over development, prototyping, qualification, and early-to-mid volume manufacturing, while partners like WIN Semiconductors provide a path to ultra-high-volume scale when needed. That combination supports the company’s shift from 1-to-1 to one-to-many: design once, qualify once, and sell repeatedly with known specs, shorter lead times, and better operating leverage. In an industry where many photonics players are fabless, owning this specialized manufacturing capability is a quiet but powerful advantage. Underrated foundation for $SIVE. text:
$AAOI is good for photonics and $CRDO for connectivity. Also $CLS and $COHR which does it all, and $AEHR for testing every semiconductor.

Software it's clearly $PLTR and $APP upside is massive as well as $NOW.

Defense - Drones are probably a better bet. I like $SWMR after the IPO.. price is more attractive with Erik Prince leading it. His connections and experience is bar none the best. Swarmer software runs on a drone as well increasing the TAM 100 fold.

Health - I was against this initially but the rise of peptides is real, so $HIMS and $IBRX for cancer in biotech.

Space - $PL (Google backed) and $RKLB is great. $ASTS is gonna have too much competition for me.

Robotics.. $TSLA - People need to start making that connection and just go long.

Insurance - I couldn't care less about. All scams. Probably United Healthcare though.

Cybersecurity is gonna be $CRWD - they're onboard for Anthropics company leadership board for security concerns around AI guardrails.

Quantum... still early for me to go all in, but $IONQ $INFQ are solid bets.

text:
No, not really similar in core tech. 

VIGO Photonics makes high-sensitivity HgCdTe (MCT) uncooled IR detectors/sensors for MWIR/LWIR detection—used in missiles, gas sensing, defense, and space (e.g. Curiosity rover). 

$LPTH focuses on IR optics (chalcogenide BlackDiamond lenses) + complete thermal imaging cameras/systems. Complementary pieces of the IR chain, not the same detection tech. 

(From the infographic + company sites)

@Frenchie_ Photonics is the angle, but the real story is aerospace.
MEMSCAP's pressure sensors go into planes and rockets - defense spending is booming and that's the actual catalyst here.

text: Photonics are taking off because most are small caps. They are easily modulated by the sudden investments that come in. Now having said that, the need for optics is REAL (reserach Ultra Rubin). Whole suupply chain is trying to figure how important they will become in the next 5 years. You need lasers but you need special materials for lasers but you also need special Co-Packaged-Optics for these to work in tandem with Silicon Chips... now you need more Pure Fiber Optics glass... now you need Optics Foundry as well. So yeah, the whole chain is moving. It's hype/real depending which company we are taking about. If psychology is what you're seeking stay invested on easy to understand optics players. I am staying with $LITE $COHR $GLW. $TSM $ASX for CPO.

text:
**Optical skyrmions and optical Hall systems (or optical/photonic Hall effects) are closely related through topological photonics, nonlinear optics, and artificial gauge fields.** Optical skyrmions are topologically protected vectorial textures in light fields (e.g., polarization, spin, or pseudospin on the Poincaré/Bloch sphere), while optical Hall systems refer to photonic setups that exhibit analogs of the classical or quantum Hall effect—often involving transverse beam deflection, nonreciprocal propagation, or spin/orbital separation driven by spin-orbit coupling or Berry curvature.

The primary connection arises in **nonlinear photonic crystals**, where engineered optical (pseudospin) skyrmions emulate magnetic skyrmion behaviors from condensed matter physics. Specifically:

- Skyrmionic textures are created in the effective “magnetization” (pseudospin) of nonlinear media via spatially varying nonlinearity (e.g., poling patterns in 3D nonlinear photonic crystals). This imposes arbitrary topological windings (including high-order skyrmions with winding number |n| > 1) that would be unstable in real magnetic materials.

- These textures generate **synthetic gauge fields** (vector and scalar potentials) via phase-matching and nonlinear coupling in three-wave mixing processes (e.g., sum-frequency generation). The propagation direction acts as “time,” and the transverse beam profile emulates 2D spin transport.

- This leads directly to emulation of the **topological Hall effect (THE)** and the **skyrmion Hall effect** for light: beams (or pseudospin currents) experience a Lorentz-like deflection proportional to the skyrmion number \(S = \frac{1}{4\pi} \int \hat{\mathbf{M}} \cdot (\partial_x \hat{\mathbf{M}} \times \partial_y \hat{\mathbf{M}}) \, d^2\mathbf{r}\). The deflection originates from real-space Berry curvature, producing an emergent magnetic field \(\mathbf{B} \propto \hat{\mathbf{M}} \cdot (\partial_x \hat{\mathbf{M}} \times \partial_y \hat{\mathbf{M}})\). Only topologically nontrivial textures (\(S \neq 0\)) cause net deflection.

Key reference: Karnieli et al. (2021) in *Nature Communications* (“Emulating spin transport with nonlinear optics, from high-order skyrmions to the topological Hall effect”) demonstrates this exactly, proposing skyrmionic nonlinear photonic crystals as a platform for all-optical control of photonic Hall phenomena.

Additional links include:
- **Hall effect-like splitting of optical Stokes skyrmions (HESS)**: Recent experiments use hybrid filters or structured light to induce symmetric/asymmetric transverse splitting of skyrmions, directly analogous to a photonic Hall effect.
- **Optical spin-orbit (or spin-Hall) effects**: Skyrmion stability in free-space or confined beams is often protected by photonic spin-orbit coupling, which itself underlies various optical Hall phenomena.
- Broader applications: These systems enable **photonic Hall devices**, topological routers, multi-level logic gates, and quantum spintronics simulations in optics—extending skyrmion Hall physics into light-based information processing.

In short, optical skyrmions provide the topological “building blocks” that create artificial gauge fields and Berry curvature landscapes, directly giving rise to controllable Hall-like transport and deflection in optical systems. This makes them a powerful tool for realizing and studying optical Hall effects with unprecedented tunability (including all-optical control via pump beams carrying orbital angular momentum).

This framework bridges condensed-matter skyrmion physics with structured light and topological photonics, as highlighted in reviews on optical skyrmions. If you have a specific paper, experimental setup, or aspect (e.g., free-space vs. nonlinear crystals) in mind, I can dive deeper!

text:
Israeli chip startup @DustPhotonics in advanced sale talks worth hundreds of millions.

Potential buyers include Intel, Nvidia, and Amazon as photonics demand surges in AI infrastructure.
https://t.co/a8803RIXSd

I think $SIVE have around 1 year runway (need to double check that) which would make a raise inevitable unless a buyout occurs first.

However, the spin off of Sivers Photonics to the US is a nice defensive play imo - allows the high growth AI unit to tap into Silicon Valley capital at much higher valuations if they need/want to.

Which could shield the parent company from a low valuation dilution.

If they raise cash via a private placement with a partner like $MRVL or $NVDA, it’s a bullish validation.

But I pretty much see the US spin-off is their main get out of jail free card.

text:
光子學：AI 革命的下一層機會

光子學（Photonics）無疑是 AI 基礎設施中最真實的趨勢之一。

隨著資料中心對更高頻寬、更低功耗的需求爆炸式增長，從 800G 到 1.6T 甚至未來的共同封裝光學（CPO），光通訊已成為連接 GPU 叢集的「神經系統」。

然而，最明顯、最直接的交易機會已經結束。

$LITE （Lumentum）、 $AAOI （Applied Optoelectronics）和  $COHR （Coherent）這些直接受益於雷射與收發器的公司，確實是瓶頸解決方案。

它們在 2026 年表現亮眼，股價大幅上漲，市場已將未來成長充分計價。這些是優質企業，但擁擠的交易、拉高的預期，讓輕鬆錢不再容易賺。

真正的機會，往往藏在供應鏈更深的一層。
例如 $MTSI （MACOM）和 $MRVL （Marvell）正安靜地從每一次頻寬升級中分得一杯羹，卻沒有那麼多市場關注。

$AEHR （Aehr Test Systems）作為測試設備的「鏟子」公司，若矽光子（SiPh）真正量產，測試需求將是不可或缺的環節，目前仍被許多人低估。

$TSEM （Tower Semiconductor）則是關鍵的製造平台——大家都在談晶片，卻很少人討論光子學實際在哪裡被生產出來。

更早期的高風險高報酬選擇如 $POET 和 $ALMU ，擁有技術潛力，但執行風險高、時間表可能延遲，需要投資人擁有足夠耐心。至於 $CIEN 、 $FN 、 $GLW  這些公司，雖然缺乏刺激的故事，卻能在光子學規模化時穩健獲利。

如果 CPO 技術真正落地，最大的上漲空間或許還在尚未上市的私人公司裡。

投資光子學的智慧不在追逐已經漲過的熱門股，而在深入供應鏈，尋找尚未被充分定價的 alpha。這正是第二層思考的價值：在眾人擁擠的表層之下，挖掘更隱蔽、卻更持久的機會。

光子學的時代才剛開始，下一波贏家，很可能來自那些再深一層的玩家。

text:
🔥🚀 AI真正的瓶颈不是算力，而是“光”——这条10层光子产业链正在重新定价一切

市场还在盯着 GPU，但真正限制 AI 扩张的，是“芯片下面那一层”。

不是算不动，而是传不动。

PHOTONICS / OPTICAL STACK — THE FULL VALUE CHAIN

“The layer below the chips” — AI’s real bottleneck

Layer 1 — Materials
Foundation of everything (InP, fiber, wafers)
$AXTI $GLW $COHR
这一层决定了整个光学世界的“原材料上限”。InP、光纤、晶圆这些看起来最基础的东西，实际上决定了后面所有性能的天花板。
Layer 2 — SiPh Foundries
Where photonic chips are fabricated
$TSMC $GFS $TSEM

硅光子代工，是把设计变成现实的入口。一旦进入这里，意味着技术从概念走向规模制造。

Layer 3 — Lasers (Bottleneck)
EML + CW lasers — tightest supply in the chain
$LITE $COHR $AAOI $FN
这是整个链条最关键的一层。
所有光互连最终都依赖“光源”。没有稳定、可规模化的激光器，后面所有层都无法成立。

这也是为什么“瓶颈=定价权”。

谁卡住这一层，谁就掌握利润分配权。
Layer 4 — PICs (Optical Chips)
Integration layer replacing discrete components
$POET $LWLG $MRVL $AVGO
这里是架构变化的核心。

从分立器件走向集成光芯片，意味着成本下降、效率提升、规模化能力增强。

$LWLG 这种材料级创新，本质是在试图改变这一层的效率函数。

Layer 5 — Transceivers
400G → 800G → 1.6T data movement
$CIEN $AAOI $LITE $COHR

真正承载数据流的模块。

速度升级路径已经很清晰，但问题在于：每一次升级，功耗和热都在同步放大。
Layer 6 — Co-Packaged Optics (CPO)
Next-gen architecture — optics + silicon combined
$NVDA $AVGO $MRVL $COHR $LITE $ALAB
这是下一代架构方向。

把光和芯片直接封装在一起，减少中间损耗，是目前最现实的优化路径之一。

但它对上游（尤其是激光器）提出更高要求。

Layer 7 — Optical Switching (OCS)
Routing light without electrical conversion
$CIEN $COHR $LITE

如果这一层成熟，数据将不再需要频繁“光电转换”。
这对延迟和能耗都是质变。
Layer 8 — Networking Systems
DCI, long-haul, metro infrastructure
$CIEN $ANET $NOK

最终把所有技术变成可运行网络的层级。

这里决定的是“能不能用”，而不是“能不能做”。

Layer 9 — Test & EDA
Hidden backbone enabling scale
$KEYS $FORM $ANSS
最容易被忽略的一层，但也是无法跳过的一层。
没有测试，就没有良率；没有良率，就没有规模。

这类公司往往在产业爆发后才被重新定价。

Layer 10 — Demand Drivers
Hyperscalers pulling the entire chain
$NVDA $META $GOOGL $MSFT $AMZN

所有需求的源头。

AI 集群扩张，正在把整条链往上拉。
THE BIG PICTURE (2026–2027)
AI 需求是指数级增长
电互连已经接近物理极限
光，是唯一可扩展路径

真正值得盯住的，不是“哪个环节最热”，而是“哪个环节最卡”。

当前最明确的约束，已经写在链条里：

Laser supply（InP → EML/CW）
这不是一个短期问题，而是一个结构性供给约束。
当需求继续上升，这一层自然会变成定价核心。

所以这不是一笔“选股交易”。

这是一次类似 2000 年代半导体的基础设施迁移。

当年是“电的时代”完成重构。
现在，是“光的时代”刚刚开始。
真正的赢家，未必是最显眼的公司。

而是那些——

让数据以光速流动成为可能的公司。

你更倾向于哪一层先被市场重新定价？

是最上层的需求驱动，还是最底层的瓶颈供给？

这一层决定了整个光学世界的“原材料上限”。InP、光纤、晶圆这些看起来最基础的东西，实际上决定了后面所有性能的天花板。 Layer 2 — SiPh Foundries Where photonic chips are fabricated $TSMC $GFS $TSEM 硅光子代工，是把设计变成现实的入口。一旦进入这里，意味着技术从概念走向规模制造。 Layer 3 — Lasers (Bottleneck) EML + CW lasers — tightest supply in the chain $LITE $COHR $AAOI $FN 这是整个链条最关键的一层。 所有光互连最终都依赖“光源”。没有稳定、可规模化的激光器，后面所有层都无法成立。 这也是为什么“瓶颈=定价权”。 谁卡住这一层，谁就掌握利润分配权。 Layer 4 — PICs (Optical Chips) Integration layer replacing discrete components $POET $LWLG $MRVL $AVGO 这里是架构变化的核心。 从分立器件走向集成光芯片，意味着成本下降、效率提升、规模化能力增强。 $LWLG 这种材料级创新，本质是在试图改变这一层的效率函数。 Layer 5 — Transceivers 400G → 800G → 1.6T data movement $CIEN $AAOI $LITE $COHR 真正承载数据流的模块。 速度升级路径已经很清晰，但问题在于：每一次升级，功耗和热都在同步放大。 Layer 6 — Co-Packaged Optics (CPO) Next-gen architecture — optics + silicon combined $NVDA $AVGO $MRVL $COHR $LITE $ALAB 这是下一代架构方向。 把光和芯片直接封装在一起，减少中间损耗，是目前最现实的优化路径之一。 但它对上游（尤其是激光器）提出更高要求。 Layer 7 — Optical Switching (OCS) Routing light without electrical conversion $CIEN $COHR $LITE 如果这一层成熟，数据将不再需要频繁“光电转换”。 这对延迟和能耗都是质变。 Layer 8 — Networking Systems DCI, long-haul, metro infrastructure $CIEN $ANET $NOK 最终把所有技术变成可运行网络的层级。 这里决定的是“能不能用”，而不是“能不能做”。 Layer 9 — Test & EDA Hidden backbone enabling scale $KEYS $FORM $ANSS 最容易被忽略的一层，但也是无法跳过的一层。 没有测试，就没有良率；没有良率，就没有规模。 这类公司往往在产业爆发后才被重新定价。 Layer 10 — Demand Drivers Hyperscalers pulling the entire chain $NVDA $META $GOOGL $MSFT $AMZN 所有需求的源头。 AI 集群扩张，正在把整条链往上拉。 THE BIG PICTURE (2026–2027) AI 需求是指数级增长 电互连已经接近物理极限 光，是唯一可扩展路径 真正值得盯住的，不是“哪个环节最热”，而是“哪个环节最卡”。 当前最明确的约束，已经写在链条里： Laser supply（InP → EML/CW） 这不是一个短期问题，而是一个结构性供给约束。 当需求继续上升，这一层自然会变成定价核心。 所以这不是一笔“选股交易”。 这是一次类似 2000 年代半导体的基础设施迁移。 当年是“电的时代”完成重构。 现在，是“光的时代”刚刚开始。 真正的赢家，未必是最显眼的公司。 而是那些—— 让数据以光速流动成为可能的公司。 你更倾向于哪一层先被市场重新定价？ 是最上层的需求驱动，还是最底层的瓶颈供给？"}}}, "rest_id": "2043367421692506300", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "471", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
Credo $CRDO getting interesting...

Credo was one of the early true market leaders of this bull cycle. 

+245% in 2024
+114% in 2025

But the market has shifted its focus to photonics plays like $LITE $COHR $CIEN etc. 

But the interconnects market won't be a winner take all. Copper-based is still needed in most AI clusters. 

CEOs of $NVDA and $AVGO had both recently confirmed this. 

Credo's underlying business is also still booming.

Its sales grew 201% last quarter. EPS grew 328%

CRDO is projected to report 153% sales and 188% when it shares its Q1 report.

TLDR: CRDO's fundamentals remain stellar.
